# Design Direction — The Postpartum OT
*Generated by Design Director — Phase 4.5*
*Build begins only after this document is complete and reviewed.*

---

## What Is Wrong With the Current Build

Before anything else — here is an honest audit of the existing homepage so the developer knows exactly what to undo:

**Hero:** Navy background. 50/50 grid. Text left, image right in a rounded rectangle box. This is the most common hero layout on the internet. Melissa's photo is boxed and constrained. It should be liberated.

**Problem Section:** Centered paragraph text. The stat "1 in 7 mothers" is buried in a paragraph. This is a display number — it should dominate a section on its own.

**Pillars:** Three equal cards in a row. Same width. Same height. Same weight. No hierarchy. Mentorship is the primary offering — you cannot tell that from the current layout.

**About Melissa:** 50/50 grid again. Image in a rounded box on the left. This is the default "meet the founder" section from every template ever made.

**Testimonials:** Three equal dark cards in a row on a navy background. A slider at some point. None of this respects the weight of what people say about transformational work.

**Rhythm:** Cream → White → Cream → Navy → Teal → White. Mechanical alternation. No emotional logic to the sequence.

**Typography:** Lato at reasonable sizes. Lora used occasionally. Nothing doing design work. Everything just labeling things.

None of this ships as-is. Everything below tells you what replaces it.

---

## Brand Personality Summary

This is a personal brand built by a single woman who has spent a decade showing up for families at their most vulnerable moment. It carries the warmth of a mentor, the credibility of a 10-year expert, and the energy of someone building a movement — not a course catalog.

The site should feel like walking into a space that was made specifically for the person reading it. Warm but precise. Intimate but authoritative. Human but professional. The design should feel like Melissa — not like "a healthcare website."

Competitors feel institutional or dated or both. This site should feel like it's from a different decade entirely.

**Energy:** 6/10 — purposeful momentum, not frenetic
**Warmth:** 9/10 — this is the dominant characteristic of every design decision
**Authority:** 8/10 — expressed through calm confidence, not visual aggression
**Formality:** 3/10 — mentor energy, not professor energy
**Visual Density:** 3/10 — emotionally heavy content demands breathing room in the design
**Risk Tolerance:** 5/10 — distinctive enough to be remembered, warm enough not to alarm

---

## Emotional Journey Map

| Page Position | Target Feeling | Visual Tool |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| Arrival (hero) | "This person sees me. I'm in the right place." | Melissa's face, full presence, warm lighting |
| First scroll | Urgency without fear — the problem is real and named | Giant display statistics as design anchors |
| Mid-page (pillars) | Clarity and possibility — "Here's how. I can do this." | Clear visual hierarchy, primary vs. secondary |
| About Melissa | Trust and intimacy — "She's been in those rooms. She knows." | Full portrait, pull quote from her voice |
| Testimonials | Belonging — "Others have done this. I'm not alone." | Large editorial quotes, real names and faces |
| Close/CTA | Warmth and readiness — "The first step is safe." | Intimate, low-pressure invitation |

The emotional arc matters. Every section transitions into the next with this arc in mind — not just with color changes.

---

## Typography System

**Heading font:** Lato — keep it. Lato Bold and Black are doing the right job: geometric authority that doesn't feel cold.

**Body font:** Lora — keep it. The serif warmth is essential to this brand. But it must do MORE design work than it currently does.

**What needs to change:** Sizes. Usage. Design intent. Currently everything is at "reasonable" sizes. We need Lato Black at display scale doing real work, and Lora Italic as a visible, weighted design element — not just body copy.

### Type Scale

| Level | Size | Weight | Line Height | Tracking | Notes |
|-------|------|--------|-------------|---------|-------|
| Display | 9vw (min 72px, max 160px) | Lato Black 900 | 0.9 | -0.03em | Hero statements only — one per site |
| Stat Display | 8vw (min 64px, max 120px) | Lato Black 900 | 0.85 | -0.04em | "1 in 7", "50%", "600+" — these are layout elements |
| H1 | 52px | Lato Black 900 | 1.05 | -0.02em | One per page |
| H2 | 38px | Lato Bold 700 | 1.15 | -0.01em | Section headings |
| H3 | 24px | Lato Bold 700 | 1.3 | 0 | Card headings, subsections |
| Lora Lede | 22px | Lora Regular Italic | 1.65 | 0 | Section intro text — the emotional hook before H2 |
| Body | 17px | Lato Regular 400 | 1.75 | 0 | Paragraphs |
| Body Lora | 17px | Lora Regular | 1.75 | 0 | Melissa's voice sections — about, testimonials |
| Pull Quote | 26px | Lora Italic | 1.5 | 0 | Featured testimonials, Melissa quotes |
| Label | 11px | Lato Bold 700 + uppercase | 1 | 0.12em | Section tags, metadata, overlines |
| Small | 14px | Lato Regular 400 | 1.6 | 0 | Captions, fine print |

### Typographic Moves Used in This Build

**1. Stat-as-design-element** (Stats section)
"1 in 7" in Lato Black at 120px in teal. "50%" offset below and to the right at 100px in navy. These numbers ARE the layout — text wraps around them. This is the most important typographic move in the build.

**2. Lora Italic lede** (Opening of most content sections)
Before each H2 section heading, a 22px Lora Italic sentence sets the emotional context. Example: *"You already know the work matters."* This becomes a signature rhythm throughout the site.

**3. Large pull quote as section anchor** (Testimonials section)
The featured testimonial quote at 28px Lora Italic with a teal left border, 4px thick, taking up 65% of section width. This is a layout element, not just a quote card.

**4. Tracked caps overline** (Before section headings throughout)
"[ Mentorship ]" or "The Work" in 11px Lato Bold uppercase with 0.12em tracking. Appears above H2s to provide editorial context. Acts as a visual rhythm element.

**5. Display headline in hero**
The hero headline is not a normal H1. It runs at 7-8vw minimum, Lato Black. "Transforming Postpartum Care" should feel large enough to be a visual shape, not just readable text.

---

## Color Architecture

| Role | Name | Hex | WCAG Status |
|------|------|-----|-------------|
| Primary background | Cream | #FEFDF8 | N/A — background |
| Secondary background | White | #FFFFFF | N/A — background |
| Brand teal | Teal | #469695 | AA on cream: PASS (4.72:1) |
| Brand navy | Navy | #0A3660 | AA on cream: PASS (11.8:1) |
| Accent warm | Sage | #7A9E7E | Use sparingly — tertiary warmth |
| Text primary | Navy | #0A3660 | PASS on cream and white |
| Text secondary | Slate | #4B5563 | PASS on cream and white |
| Text on dark | White | #FFFFFF | PASS on navy and teal |
| Interactive | Teal | #469695 | Verified on cream |
| Interactive hover | Deep Teal | #357878 | PASS |

**Verify all combinations at:** https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/

### Section Color Sequence (Homepage)

This replaces the mechanical cream/white/cream/navy/teal/white rhythm:

| # | Section | Background | Rationale |
|---|---------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Hero | Cream #FEFDF8 + Melissa photo | Opens with warmth, her face is the dominant element |
| 2 | Stats | Cream #FEFDF8 | Continuous with hero — no hard break, the numbers land in the same warm space |
| 3 | Pillars | White #FFFFFF | Slight lift — clarity and focus for the "what" section |
| 4 | About Melissa | Teal #469695 | Color shift creates an emotional pause — most intimate section gets the brand color |
| 5 | Testimonials | Cream #FEFDF8 | Return to warmth — belonging feels warm, not institutional |
| 6 | Email Capture | Navy #0A3660 | Authority close — the invitation is serious and certain |
| 7 | Podcast | White #FFFFFF | Light palate cleanser, editorial feel |

**The logic:** The site moves from warmth → urgency → clarity → intimacy → belonging → authority → exploration. Each color choice reinforces the emotion of that moment.

### Color as Design Tool

The About Melissa section background is teal (#469695). The text on it is white. Melissa's portrait bleeds off the right edge. This is a full-bleed teal section — not a box, not a card. The entire section is her color. This is one of the most important design moments on the site.

---

## Layout Architecture

**Grid system:** Hybrid — some sections use structured grids for clarity; hero and about sections use broken/fluid layouts where image bleeds and elements escape the container.

**Container behavior:** Content constrained to max 1280px with 24-40px side padding. Some elements (primarily photography) intentionally escape the container and bleed to the viewport edge.

**Asymmetry strategy:** Column ratio asymmetry throughout. No 50/50 layouts anywhere except where symmetry is deliberately used to create stillness (not present on homepage).

---

## Section-by-Section Layout

### Hero

**What it is NOT:** Navy background, 50/50 grid, rounded image box on the right.

**What it IS:**
- Cream (#FEFDF8) background
- Layout: 38/62 split. Left 38% contains headline, subheadline, two CTAs on cream. Right 62% is Melissa's portrait photo — no rounded corners, no box, bleeds to the browser's right edge and top/bottom of section.
- The photo acts as the right wall of the browser.
- A subtle warm gradient on the left edge of the photo (cream → transparent, 80px wide) creates the merge between text zone and image.
- Hero section height: min-height 90vh
- Typography: "Transforming Postpartum Care" at 7vw Lato Black, Navy. Line break after "Care —". Second line "One Clinician at a Time" in teal.
- Subheadline: 20px Lora Italic, slate gray, below headline with 24px gap
- CTAs: Two pill-shaped buttons. Primary: teal fill, white text. Secondary: cream background, navy border, navy text. Side by side with 16px gap.
- Decorative: Small tracked-caps overline above headline — "[ The Postpartum OT ]" in 11px navy at 0.12em tracking.

**Visual anchor:** Melissa's eyes in the photograph.
**Eye movement:** Headline → Melissa's face → subheadline → CTAs

---

### Stats Section

**What it is NOT:** Centered paragraph text. "1 in 7" buried in a sentence.

**What it IS:**
- Cream background, continuous with hero (no color break)
- No section heading. The numbers ARE the heading.
- Layout: Two massive display numbers creating the layout structure. "1 in 7" in Lato Black at 120px teal, positioned left-aligned at full container width. "50%" in Lato Black at 100px navy, positioned right-aligned, 40px lower than the first (staggered vertical position — this is the key move).
- Between and below them: short explanatory copy in 17px Lato, slate gray, max-width 520px, positioned to thread between the numbers.
- A single 2px teal horizontal rule, 40px wide, sits above the section as an intro marker.
- Bottom of section: "And after a single 6-week checkup, most families are left to figure it out alone." in 22px Lora Italic centered, max-width 640px. This is the emotional landing of the stats section.
- Ample padding: 100px top and bottom minimum.

**Visual anchor:** The "1 in 7" number — it's enormous, it lands first.
**Eye movement:** "1 in 7" → "50%" → connecting text → Lora italic closing line

---

### Pillars Section (What We Do)

**What it is NOT:** Three equal-width, equal-height cards in a row.

**What it IS:**
- White background
- Section overline: "[ The Work ]" in tracked caps above the H2
- H2: "Everything You Need to Build a Postpartum Practice" — left aligned, max-width 600px
- Layout: Asymmetric 55/45 split.
  - LEFT (55%): The FEATURED PILLAR — Mentorship. This is a tall card that takes up the full height of the section. It has a teal background, white text, large Lucide BookOpen icon at 48px, H3 at full weight, body text, and a full CTA button. This card is DOMINANT. It visually reads as "this is the main thing."
  - RIGHT (45%): Two stacked secondary pillars — The Collective (top) and Podcast (bottom). These are cream background, navy text, smaller icon at 32px. They are clearly supporting players.
- The featured card on the left is at least 1.5x the visual weight of each secondary card.
- On mobile: Featured card full-width first, then secondary cards stacked below.

**Visual anchor:** The teal featured Mentorship card on the left.
**Eye movement:** Mentorship card → Collective → Podcast

---

### About Melissa Section

**What it is NOT:** 50/50 grid with Melissa's photo in a rounded box on the left.

**What it IS:**
- Teal (#469695) background, full bleed
- Layout: 45/55 split. LEFT (45%): text content on teal. RIGHT (55%): Melissa's portrait photo, cropped vertically (portrait orientation), bleeding to the browser's right edge and to the top and bottom of the section. No rounded corners. No box. The photo IS the right wall of this section.
- The text zone (left 45%) contains:
  - Tracked-caps overline: "[ Meet Melissa ]"
  - H2 "Meet Melissa" in white, Lato Black, 38px
  - A single Lora Italic pull quote at 22px in white/90 opacity: *"I couldn't reach every family myself. The need was too great."*
  - Body text in Lora Regular 17px, white/85 opacity — 2 paragraphs max
  - Button: cream fill, navy text (inverted from the standard — honors the teal background)
- Padding left: generous — 80px minimum from the left edge of the container, creating breathing room between the container edge and text.

**Image choice:** `melissa-oneal-portrait.jpg` or `melissa-about-page.jpg` — vertical crop. She should be looking toward the text zone (into the page, not away from it).

**Visual anchor:** Melissa's portrait — she draws the eye immediately on a teal field.
**Eye movement:** Melissa's face → overline → H2 → pull quote → body → CTA

---

### Testimonials Section

**What it is NOT:** Three equal navy cards with placeholder text.

**What it IS (when testimonials arrive from client):**
- Cream (#FEFDF8) background
- Section overline: "[ What Clinicians Are Saying ]"
- Layout: Editorial testimonials grid — NOT equal cards, NOT a slider.
  - ONE featured testimonial at the top: Full-width or 75% width. Background: white. Left border: 4px solid teal. Large Lora Italic pull quote at 28px navy, max-width 680px. Attribution below in 14px Lato Bold — name, credential, context.
  - TWO secondary testimonials below: Side by side (50/50 this one time — deliberate symmetry for balance), smaller, 16px Lora Italic quote text, same left border treatment at 3px.
- No cards with equal rounded corners and equal heights. Each quote is sized to its content.
- Hover: subtle cream-to-white background transition on each secondary testimonial. 200ms.

**Placeholder until testimonials arrive:** Do not use placeholder cards. Use this section to display one of Melissa's own quotes from the content doc in the same featured format. It reads as genuine until real testimonials replace it.

---

### Email Capture Section

**What it is:**
- Navy (#0A3660) background — authoritative close
- Layout: Two-column 35/65.
  - LEFT (35%): Small Melissa headshot in a soft circle (60px diameter), name "Melissa O'Neal, OTR/L" in white small text beneath. This makes the invitation personal — it's from a person, not a brand.
  - RIGHT (65%): H2 in white Lato Black, 32px. Lora Italic intro in white/85 opacity beneath. Form: single email input (cream background, navy text) + pill CTA button (teal fill, white text). Fine print in 12px white/60 opacity beneath.
- This section currently works structurally but lacks the personal touch. The portrait element fixes that.

---

### Podcast Section

**What it is NOT:** Centered iframe embed on white background.

**What it IS:**
- White background
- Layout: 50/50 — LEFT contains the episode visual (show artwork or a warm photography frame), RIGHT contains the section context and Spotify embed.
- Left side: Large podcast artwork image (square, 1:1) at full container left half width. Soft rounded corners on this one — 12px — because it's a media object, not a landscape photo.
- Right side: Section overline "[ Functional Fourth Trimester ]", H2 "Real Conversations About Postpartum Care", Lora Italic intro sentence, Spotify iframe embed, two CTA buttons stacked (Listen on Spotify, See All Episodes).
- The artwork is a design element here — it gives the section a visual anchor without needing photography.

---

## Whitespace System

**Between sections:** 96px top/bottom padding on desktop, 64px on mobile. This is the baseline.

**Exceptions:**
- Hero → Stats: 80px top padding on Stats (slightly less — they're emotionally continuous)
- Stats section: 120px top and bottom (the numbers need room to breathe)
- About Melissa section: 0px top/bottom padding — the teal section is full-bleed and photo-based; padding lives inside the text zone, not the section

**Isolation moments:**
- The Lora italic closing line in the Stats section ("And after a single 6-week checkup...") is centered with 60px of vertical whitespace above it — it is isolated to create emotional weight.
- The featured testimonial pull quote has 40px above and below it before the secondary quotes appear.
- The CTA buttons in the Hero have 32px of space above them from the subheadline.

---

## Shape Language

**Primary personality:** Organic with restraint. Warmth through soft corners and occasional blob shapes, but anchored by strong grid decisions underneath.

**Border radius system:**
| Element | Radius |
|---------|--------|
| Secondary pillar cards | 16px |
| Featured Mentorship card | 20px |
| Email input | 9999px (full pill) |
| CTA buttons | 9999px (full pill) |
| Podcast artwork | 12px |
| Secondary testimonial containers | 0px — just the left border |
| Photography (hero, about) | 0px — bleeds to edge |
| Headshot circles | 50% |
| Tags / labels / badges | 9999px (full pill) |

**Decorative shape usage:**

1. **Organic blob behind Stats section numbers** — a very subtle, low-opacity (#469695 at 5% opacity) blob shape sits behind the stat numbers as a background texture element. Not obvious. Just enough to ground the numbers.

2. **Wave divider between Stats and Pillars** — a gentle SVG wave (not a hard line) transitions the cream Stats section to the white Pillars section. The wave is subtle — 1 wavelength, cream on white.

3. **Decorative circle accent in Hero** — a 120px circle, teal at 15% opacity, positioned in the bottom-left corner of the text zone in the hero. Partially cut off by the left edge. Adds organic softness without competing with the headline.

4. **No blobs anywhere that are obvious** — the organic shapes are texture, not decoration. They support the layout; they don't become the layout.

---

## Imagery Direction

**Photography personality:** Lifestyle/portrait hybrid. Real moments, natural light, Melissa in her element — or close to it. Not staged, not stock, not clinical.

**Image treatment:** Warm film. Slightly increased contrast in the warm tones. A very slight warm wash to the shadows. This is a CSS filter or a pre-applied Lightroom preset. Consistency across all images is mandatory.

Tailwind CSS filter for all photos: `brightness-95 saturate-90` or a custom CSS class:
```css
.photo-warm {
  filter: brightness(0.97) contrast(1.05) saturate(0.9) sepia(0.06);
}
```

**Layout interaction:**
- Hero and About Melissa: Photo bleeds to browser edge (right side). Zero rounded corners. This is the signature image treatment.
- Pillar cards: No photography — icons only. Photography would compete with the card hierarchy.
- Podcast: Artwork in a square with 12px radius — media object treatment.
- Blog previews: 16:9 landscape, 12px radius, all same size.
- Team/Melissa in body content: Portrait crop, soft circle or 1:1 square with 12px radius.

**Aspect ratios:**
| Context | Ratio | Notes |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Hero photo | Full height of hero section, fluid width | Bleeds to right browser edge |
| About Melissa portrait | Portrait: 2:3 ratio | Bleeds to right browser edge |
| Blog thumbnails | 16:9 | Consistent across all cards |
| Podcast artwork | 1:1 | Square |
| Melissa headshot (email section) | 1:1 circle | 60px diameter |

**Image sources:**
All Melissa photos are in `assets/images/` — use existing assets. Preferred for hero: `melissa-hero-1.jpg` or `melissa-hero-2.jpg` (whichever has warmer lighting and shows her face clearly). Preferred for about section: `melissa-oneal-portrait.jpg`.

---

## Motion System

**Philosophy:** Motion should feel inevitable, not decorative. Every animation serves the emotional journey — it doesn't just show off.

**Scroll reveal approach:** Staggered fade-up with directional logic. Elements that are positioned left come from the left slightly. Elements positioned right come from the right. Vertical-stack elements reveal top-to-bottom with 80ms stagger between each.

**Library:** Framer Motion — `npm install framer-motion`

**Hero entrance sequence (on page load, not scroll):**
1. Melissa's photo: fades in from scale(1.04) to scale(1.0) over 800ms, ease-out
2. Overline text: fades in from opacity 0 at 200ms delay, 400ms duration
3. Headline: fades up (y: 16px → 0) at 400ms delay, 500ms duration
4. Subheadline: fades up at 600ms delay, 400ms duration
5. CTA buttons: fades up at 750ms delay, 400ms duration, staggered 100ms between them

**Stat number entrance (scroll-triggered):**
"1 in 7" and "50%" — count-up animation. From 0 to final value over 1200ms with ease-out. This is the most impactful motion in the build. Use `framer-motion` motion value or `react-countup` library.

**Standard section reveals:**
- Y: 24px → 0, opacity: 0 → 1, duration: 350ms, ease-out
- Stagger between children: 80ms
- Trigger: when element is 15% into viewport

**Hover states:**

| Element | Hover Behavior | Duration |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| CTA buttons (primary) | Background darkens 15% (teal → deep teal) | 200ms |
| CTA buttons (ghost) | Fill slides in from left with clip-path | 200ms |
| Secondary pillar cards | translateY(-4px), box-shadow increase | 200ms |
| Featured Mentorship card | Very subtle: border-left brightens | 200ms |
| Navigation links | Teal underline draws in from left | 200ms |
| Blog card images | scale(1.03) within container | 300ms |
| Text arrow links (→) | Arrow slides 4px to the right | 150ms |

**Page transitions:** None. This client base (clinicians, new moms) does not need decorative page transitions. Clean and instant is faster and more respectful of their time.

**Reduced motion:** All animations must respect `prefers-reduced-motion`. Wrap all Framer Motion components with the `useReducedMotion` hook and disable or simplify animations when active.

```tsx
const shouldReduceMotion = useReducedMotion();
const variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0, y: shouldReduceMotion ? 0 : 24 },
  visible: { opacity: 1, y: 0 },
};
```

**Animation duration reference:**
| Type | Duration |
|------|---------|
| Micro (hover) | 150–200ms |
| Standard (section reveal) | 300–400ms |
| Hero entrance | 400–800ms (staggered sequence) |
| Stat count-up | 1200ms |
| Max anywhere | 1000ms (nothing slower unless intentional) |

---

## Anti-Pattern Signoffs

These are confirmed CLEAR for the TPPOT build:

- [x] Hero: NOT image-right, text-left in a box. Melissa's photo bleeds to the browser edge on the right. Cream background on the left.
- [x] Features: NOT three equal cards. Asymmetric 55/45 with a dominant featured pillar.
- [x] Columns: NOT 50/50 (hero is 38/62, about is 45/55, pillars are 55/45). The only 50/50 is the deliberate symmetry in secondary testimonials.
- [x] Section rhythm: NOT mechanical cream/white alternation. Color choices follow the emotional arc.
- [x] Testimonials: NOT equal slider cards. Editorial hierarchy — one large featured quote + two supporting.
- [x] Buttons: NOT generic blue. Teal fill, pill shape, Lato Bold.
- [x] Typography: Stats are design elements. Lora Italic is a visible design move. Tracked caps overlines create editorial rhythm.
- [x] Images: NOT all in boxes. Hero and about photos bleed to the browser edge. No rounded corners on major photos.
- [x] Typography scale: Display text at 7-8vw for hero, stat numbers at 120px. Not "reasonable" sizes.

## Section Reference Gallery

These are not sites to copy. They represent the specific feeling we're going for in each section.

### Hero (photo-forward, editorial, personal brand)
- **Linear.app** (linear.app) — how a photo-dominant hero can feel authoritative without being stock
- **Notion** (notion.so) — big type, breathing room, cream-adjacent palette
- **Kaleigh Moore portfolio** (kaleighmoore.com) — personal brand done with editorial warmth

### Stats Section (numbers as layout elements)
- **Stripe** (stripe.com/about) — how large numbers create layout structure without being cold
- **Pitch.com** (pitch.com) — stats with emotional weight, not just data

### Asymmetric Pillars / Feature Cards
- **Superfluid** (superfluid.finance) — asymmetric card weight in feature sections
- **Raycast** (raycast.com) — featured element vs. supporting elements, very clear hierarchy

### About / Founder Section (photo bleeding to edge)
- **James Clear** (jamesclear.com/about) — personal brand with full presence, not boxed
- **Spring Health** (springhealth.com) — healthcare with warmth, no clinical coldness

### Testimonials (editorial, pull quotes as design)
- **Figma.com** (figma.com) — how testimonials can carry real visual weight
- **Lessonly** (lessonly.com) — human testimonials, not card slots

### Typography / Editorial Rhythm
- **Typewolf "Sites I Like"** (typewolf.com) — Lora italic used as design element
- **Fraunces specimen** (fonts.google.com/specimen/Fraunces) — editorial serif at scale

---

## Developer Handoff Notes

**Read this section before opening your IDE.**

The design direction above is final. These are not suggestions. If you find something is harder to implement than the default, the answer is to implement it correctly — not to revert to a simpler layout.

**Non-negotiables — these cannot change:**

1. **Melissa's photo bleeds to the right browser edge in both the hero and about sections.** No rounded corners. No box. The image is a wall, not a frame. Use `position: absolute`, `right: 0`, and overflow on the parent section.

2. **Stats are display-scale typography — not embedded in paragraphs.** "1 in 7" is 120px Lato Black. If it's smaller than that or inside a sentence, it's wrong.

3. **Pillars section has a dominant featured card (Mentorship).** It is NOT three equal columns. The featured card is at minimum 1.4x the visual weight of each secondary card.

4. **About Melissa section is teal background.** NOT cream, NOT white, NOT navy. The brand color owns that section.

5. **Pill-shape buttons everywhere.** border-radius: 9999px on all CTAs. No square buttons, no slightly-rounded buttons.

6. **Stat count-up animation is mandatory.** The numbers should animate on scroll. Use `react-countup` or Framer Motion values. This is not optional polish — it's a core experience moment.

7. **Tracked caps overlines appear above every H2 section heading.** Format: `[ Section Tag ]` in 11px Lato Bold uppercase with letter-spacing 0.12em, teal or navy depending on section background. This is the editorial rhythm signature of this site.

8. **Warm photo filter applied to all Melissa photography.** The CSS class `.photo-warm` with `filter: brightness(0.97) contrast(1.05) saturate(0.9) sepia(0.06)` must be applied to every `<Image>` component showing Melissa.

**Acceptable developer flexibility:**

- Mobile layout: Single column, stacked. The asymmetric layouts collapse gracefully. On mobile, the hero photo moves below the text (full-width, aspect-ratio 3:2). On mobile, about Melissa photo moves above the text section (full width).
- The exact blob shape and positioning for the stats background texture — the key is that it exists and is subtle. The exact organic form is up to you.
- Blog card layout on the homepage — a 3-column equal grid is acceptable here because blog cards are genuinely uniform content.
- The wave divider between stats and pillars can be simplified to a subtle edge if the SVG wave proves difficult — but do not use a hard line or no divider.

**Implementation order:**

Build in this order to catch design issues early:
1. globals.css — type scale CSS custom properties, photo-warm filter class, tracked-caps label class
2. Hero section (the most complex, the most important)
3. Stats section (the most typographically ambitious)
4. Pillars section (asymmetric layout)
5. About Melissa section (teal + bleeding photo)
6. Testimonials section (editorial hierarchy)
7. Email capture section
8. Podcast section
9. Footer
10. Then replicate relevant patterns to interior pages

**Test on real viewport widths:** 375px, 768px, 1280px, 1440px, 1920px. The bleeding photography must work at every width. The display stat numbers must scale correctly at every width using clamp() or vw units.

---

## Design Quality Gate

Before showing Tyler:

- [ ] Hero photo bleeds to right browser edge with no rounded corners or box
- [ ] Hero column ratio is approximately 38/62
- [ ] Stat numbers are display-scale (100px+ on desktop)
- [ ] Stat numbers animate on scroll (count-up)
- [ ] Pillars section has one dominant featured card, not three equal columns
- [ ] About Melissa section has teal background
- [ ] About Melissa photo bleeds to right browser edge
- [ ] All buttons are pill-shaped (border-radius: 9999px)
- [ ] Tracked caps overlines appear above all H2 section headings
- [ ] Warm photo filter applied to all Melissa photography
- [ ] Lora Italic at 22px appears as section intro text (lede) in at least 3 sections
- [ ] Section color sequence follows: Cream → Cream → White → Teal → Cream → Navy → White
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` respected in all animations
- [ ] All WCAG contrast ratios pass AA (white on teal, white on navy, navy on cream)
- [ ] No 50/50 column layouts except deliberate secondary testimonial symmetry
- [ ] No three-equal-anything layouts anywhere
